slides.heading: Hunter-gatherers and Herders in Southern Africa
slides.lead: Meet the San and the Khoikhoi - the first people to live in southern Africa - and find out how keeping animals changed the way people lived.

image.alt: A dry southern African landscape with a rocky hill, an antelope, and the sun.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='258' cy='46' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect y='118' width='320' height='62' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M0 118 L60 96 L110 114 L168 86 L232 110 L320 92 L320 118 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M40 96 L70 60 L100 96 Z' fill='#9a7b53'/><g fill='#5c4327'><rect x='150' y='120' width='4' height='16'/><rect x='170' y='120' width='4' height='16'/><path d='M146 108 q12 -10 30 0 l0 14 -30 0 Z'/><path d='M146 108 l-8 -8 4 -1 6 6 Z'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Welcome learners to the story of the earliest people in southern Africa. Explain the two big groups they will meet: the San, who were hunter-gatherers, and the Khoikhoi, who were herders. Ask what they think 'hunter-gatherer' and 'herder' might mean before you begin. [SME] Dates for these societies are given as rough estimates suitable for Grade 5.

slides.heading: Who lived here first?
slides.lead: The San were the first people to live in southern Africa, many thousands of years ago.

blocks.text: The **San** were the earliest people in southern Africa. They lived here long before towns, farms or writing. This time is called the **Later Stone Age**, because people made their tools from stone, wood and bone.

items: The San did not build permanent towns - they moved from place to place.
They lived off the land by **hunting** animals and **gathering** wild plants.
Much later, another group called the Khoikhoi arrived with animals to herd.

slides.notes: Set the scene with a sense of deep time. Grade 5 learners find 'thousands of years ago' hard to picture, so compare it to many, many great-grandparents ago. Introduce the word 'nomadic' (moving around) which comes up again. Explain that we learn about these people from things they left behind, like tools and rock paintings.

slides.heading: The San: hunters and gatherers
slides.lead: The San got everything they needed from the natural world around them.

blocks.text: The San survived by **hunting** wild animals like gemsbok and other antelope, and by **gathering** wild plants, roots, berries and honey. Because they followed the animals and the seasons, they moved from place to place. We call this a **nomadic** way of life.

items: The **men** usually did the hunting.
The **women** gathered plants and dug up roots to eat.
They took only what they needed, so the land could recover.

image.alt: A hunter figure with a bow beside a woman figure carrying a digging stick and a plant.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><circle cx='70' cy='44' r='12'/><rect x='63' y='58' width='14' height='46'/><rect x='60' y='104' width='7' height='30'/><rect x='73' y='104' width='7' height='30'/></g><path d='M92 40 q22 34 0 68' fill='none' stroke='#5c4327' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='92' y1='40' x2='92' y2='108' stroke='#5c4327' stroke-width='2'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='160' cy='44' r='12'/><rect x='153' y='58' width='14' height='46'/><rect x='150' y='104' width='7' height='30'/><rect x='163' y='104' width='7' height='30'/></g><line x1='178' y1='40' x2='178' y2='120' stroke='#5c4327' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='188' cy='70' r='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the difference between hunting and gathering, and that both were needed to survive - gathering plants often gave the most reliable food. Note the sharing of tasks between men and women without judging it by today's ideas. Reinforce the respect the San had for nature: they used the land carefully so it would not be used up.

slides.heading: The bow and the poisoned arrow
slides.lead: The San invented a clever way to hunt animals much bigger than themselves.

blocks.text: The San made a small **bow and arrows**. They put **poison** on the arrow tips. After shooting an animal, they would quietly follow its tracks - sometimes for days - until the poison made it weak enough to catch. Good tracking skills were very important.

items: The poison came from plants, snakes or beetle grubs.
Hunters had to get very close and shoot only once.
They read tracks in the sand to follow the animal.

image.alt: A bow with an arrow, and a line of animal footprints in the sand.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M50 30 q40 50 0 100' fill='none' stroke='#5c4327' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='50' y1='30' x2='50' y2='130' stroke='#8a7050' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='42' y1='80' x2='150' y2='80' stroke='#5c4327' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M150 80 l-12 -6 0 12 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#9a7b53'><ellipse cx='120' cy='120' rx='5' ry='7'/><ellipse cx='140' cy='128' rx='5' ry='7'/><ellipse cx='162' cy='120' rx='5' ry='7'/><ellipse cx='184' cy='128' rx='5' ry='7'/><ellipse cx='206' cy='120' rx='5' ry='7'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This slide shows San skill and knowledge, not just survival. Emphasise the patience and deep understanding of nature it took to track an animal for days and to know which plants made poison. Handle 'poison' plainly and factually - it was a tool for finding food, not for harming people. Ask learners why shooting only once mattered (staying hidden, saving arrows).

slides.heading: San tools and everyday things
slides.lead: The San made everything they owned from what they found around them.

blocks.heading: Fire sticks
blocks.text: Two sticks rubbed together to make a spark. Fire gave warmth, light and cooked food.

blocks.heading: Ostrich eggshell
blocks.text: An empty ostrich egg was used to carry and store water, buried to keep it cool.

blocks.heading: Digging stick
blocks.text: A sharpened stick used to dig up roots and bulbs to eat.

blocks.heading: Kaross
blocks.text: A leather cloak made from an animal skin, worn for warmth and to carry things.

slides.notes: Use these four objects to make San life concrete. Point out that everything was light and could be carried, because the San were always moving. Beads and ostrich-eggshell were also used for decoration and were given as gifts to build friendships between groups. Invite learners to imagine owning only what they could carry.

slides.heading: The San shared everything
slides.lead: The San lived in small groups where everyone was equal.

blocks.text: The San lived in small **bands** of about 15 to 40 people, often a few families together. They had **no chiefs** and no one was richer or more important than anyone else. They **shared** their food and helped each other. They believed no one could own the land - it was for everyone to use.

items: Women could build a grass shelter in less than an hour.
Children learned by playing and copying their parents.
Sharing meant no one went hungry while others had food.

image.alt: A dome-shaped grass shelter next to a small campfire.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M40 122 a54 54 0 0 1 108 0 Z' fill='#9a8a5c'/><path d='M52 122 a42 42 0 0 1 84 0' fill='none' stroke='#7a6a3c' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M70 122 a26 26 0 0 1 48 0' fill='#5c4a24'/><g><path d='M186 122 l-8 -22 16 0 Z' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M186 122 l-14 -14 8 -4 Z' fill='#c8611f'/><path d='M186 122 l14 -14 -8 -4 Z' fill='#c8611f'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This is a good place to talk about fairness and community. Contrast gently with today's world where people own things. Explain that having 'no chiefs' did not mean no rules - the group made decisions together and elders were respected. Keep the tone respectful and avoid making San life sound 'simple' or 'less advanced'; it was well suited to their world.

slides.heading: San rock art
slides.lead: The San painted and carved thousands of pictures on rocks and cave walls.

blocks.text: **Rock art** means pictures painted or carved onto rock. South Africa has some of the finest rock art in the world, and most of it was made by the San. They painted people and animals like the eland, using paints made from natural colours. Some paintings in the **uKhahlamba Drakensberg** are about 3 000 years old.

items: Paint was made from things like red and yellow rock, charcoal and blood.
The **eland**, a large antelope, was painted more than any other animal.
Rock art helps us learn how the San lived and what they believed.

image.alt: A cave wall with red San-style paintings of an eland and running human figures.

slides.notes: Highlight that rock art is a real record left by the San themselves - a link to the past. Mention the eland's special importance. The famous Linton Panel (now in a museum) even inspired part of South Africa's coat of arms. [SME] The exact age of rock art varies by site; 3 000 years is the confirmed figure SAHO gives for the Drakensberg, but many paintings are younger and a few older - confirm phrasing with an SME.

slides.heading: Why did the San paint?
slides.lead: Rock art was important to San beliefs, not just decoration.

blocks.text: We cannot be completely sure why the San painted, but many experts believe the pictures were part of their **beliefs and spiritual life**. Special people called healers held dances to help their community, and some paintings may show what they felt and saw. The art also tells us about the animals and life the San knew well.

items: Some paintings may be linked to healing dances and dreams.
Others may have been made to bring good luck for a hunt.
Today rock art is protected as part of South Africa's heritage.

slides.notes: Present the spiritual meaning of rock art respectfully and simply. Make clear to learners that this is what many experts think, not a proven fact - historians use clues to make careful guesses. Avoid sensational language about 'trance' with Grade 5; 'healing dances' and 'dreams' are gentle, accurate ways in. Stress that damaging rock art is against the law because it belongs to everyone. [SME] The shamanistic/trance interpretation is the leading scholarly theory but is debated - keep it framed as 'many experts believe'.

slides.heading: The Khoikhoi herders arrive
slides.lead: About 2 000 years ago a new group brought animals to southern Africa.

blocks.text: The **Khoikhoi** moved into southern Africa from further north around **2 000 years ago**. The name *Khoikhoi* means 'the real people'. They were **herders**, also called **pastoralists**, because they kept and looked after animals. This was a brand new way of living that the San had not seen before.

items: Herders keep **domesticated** (tamed) animals instead of only hunting.
Like the San, the Khoikhoi were nomadic and moved to find grazing.
They kept sheep, goats and cattle.

image.alt: A herder figure with a stick walking beside cattle and sheep.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='122' width='240' height='38' fill='#7a8b5a'/><g fill='#5c4327'><circle cx='40' cy='52' r='11'/><rect x='34' y='64' width='12' height='40'/><rect x='31' y='104' width='6' height='24'/><rect x='43' y='104' width='6' height='24'/></g><line x1='52' y1='44' x2='52' y2='120' stroke='#8a7050' stroke-width='3'/><g fill='#8a6a44'><ellipse cx='120' cy='96' rx='26' ry='16'/><rect x='100' y='104' width='5' height='18'/><rect x='134' y='104' width='5' height='18'/><path d='M96 90 l-8 -6 8 0 Z'/></g><g fill='#e8e2d2'><ellipse cx='190' cy='104' rx='18' ry='12'/><rect x='178' y='112' width='4' height='12'/><rect x='198' y='112' width='4' height='12'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Introduce the big change of this unit: herding. Explain 'domesticated' with everyday examples (a tame dog or a farm cow versus a wild animal). Note that the Khoikhoi still hunted and gathered too, but keeping animals was the new and important difference. [SME] The origin and exact arrival date of herding/the Khoikhoi in southern Africa is debated by archaeologists; '2 000 years ago' follows the SAHO Grade 5 material and is a simplification.

slides.heading: Life with herds of animals
slides.lead: The Khoikhoi's whole life was built around their animals.

blocks.text: The Khoikhoi moved with their herds to find fresh grass and water. Their homes, called **matjieshuise**, were made of reed mats over thin poles so they could be taken down and carried on the animals' backs. **Cattle** were very precious. The women milked the animals and the men still hunted for meat.

items: Homes and pots were made light so they were easy to move.
They mostly drank the **milk** rather than killing their cattle.
Cattle were only eaten at special times, like weddings and funerals.

image.alt: A dome-shaped reed-mat house next to a cow.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='122' width='240' height='38' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M30 122 a52 52 0 0 1 104 0 Z' fill='#cdae74'/><path d='M46 122 a36 36 0 0 1 72 0' fill='none' stroke='#a88a4c' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M64 122 a18 18 0 0 1 36 0' fill='#8a6a3c'/><line x1='30' y1='108' x2='134' y2='108' stroke='#a88a4c' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='92' x2='124' y2='92' stroke='#a88a4c' stroke-width='1.5'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><ellipse cx='188' cy='96' rx='26' ry='16'/><rect x='168' y='104' width='5' height='18'/><rect x='202' y='104' width='5' height='18'/><path d='M164 90 l-8 -6 8 0 Z'/><path d='M162 84 l-4 -8 6 4 Z'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the practical cleverness of the matjieshuis - a home you can pack up and carry. The key idea is that cattle were kept alive for milk and as wealth, not mainly for meat. This is a big difference from the San, who ate the animals they hunted. Introduce the word 'matjieshuis' gently; the pronunciation is roughly 'matchie-hoyse'.

slides.heading: How herding changed society
slides.lead: Owning animals made Khoikhoi society different from San society.

items: Shared everything equally.
No chiefs; everyone was equal.
No one owned the land.
Got food by hunting and gathering.
Animals could be **owned** by a person.
Some people became **rich** and others poor.
Rich people had more **importance** and power.
Got food mainly from their herds.

slides.notes: This is the heart of the CAPS question 'how herding changed societies'. The main idea: because animals could be owned, wealth appeared, and with wealth came differences in importance and the beginnings of leaders. Draw out that this is not 'better' or 'worse' - just a different way of living that grew from keeping animals. Have learners point to one difference in each column.

slides.heading: Sharing the same land
slides.lead: The San and Khoikhoi lived close together, which sometimes caused conflict.

blocks.text: The San and Khoikhoi lived in the same areas. The Khoikhoi's cattle and sheep ate a lot of grass, and this drove away the wild animals the San hunted. When the San grew hungry, they sometimes took the Khoikhoi's animals for food. This led to fighting between the two groups. But there was also **trading** and **friendship**, and some San joined Khoikhoi groups.

items: Herds ate the grass, so wild animals moved away.
The two groups both traded with and fought each other.
Together they are sometimes called the **Khoisan**.

slides.notes: Give a balanced picture: relations were not only conflict. There was trade, intermarriage and cooperation as well as fighting over resources. Explain the combined term 'Khoisan'. This slide also gently sets up later history (the arrival of the Dutch in 1652), which learners will meet in more detail in later grades. Keep the tone factual and avoid casting either group as 'bad'.

slides.heading: A very long story

blocks.alt: A timeline from the earliest San hunter-gatherers to the arrival of the Khoikhoi herders.

events.label: San hunter-gatherers live across southern Africa
San paint rock art in the Drakensberg
San and Khoikhoi trade, share land and sometimes fight

slides.notes: Use this to help learners order the story: hunter-gatherers first, herders later. Remind them these are rough dates, not exact ones - historians use 'about' when they cannot be certain. [SME] All four dates here are approximate and simplified for Grade 5; confirm the framing with an SME.

slides.heading: What we learned
slides.lead: The San hunted and gathered and shared everything; the Khoikhoi kept herds, and owning animals changed how people lived.

blocks.text: The **San** were southern Africa's first people. They lived by **hunting and gathering**, moved from place to place, shared everything, and left us beautiful **rock art**. The **Khoikhoi** were **herders** who kept animals. Because animals could be owned, some people became richer than others - so herding changed the way society worked.

blocks.question: Name two ways the San got their food.
What is one difference between a hunter-gatherer and a herder?
How did keeping animals change Khoikhoi society? Give one example.

slides.notes: Recap the two ways of life and the key idea of change: owning animals led to wealth and differences between people. Use the three questions as a written task or class discussion. Question 1 checks recall (hunting, gathering), question 2 checks understanding of the two lifestyles, and question 3 targets the CAPS focus on how herding changed societies. Remind learners that rock art is a precious record we must protect.
